curriculumRef.value: NCE Chemistry Year 1 — CHE 111 General Chemistry

title: The Periodic Table and Chemical Bonding

blocks.title: The Modern Periodic Table

citations.excerpt: The modern periodic table is organized into horizontal rows called periods and vertical columns called groups. There are seven periods, corresponding to the filling of the seven principal electron shells.

blocks.body:
## The Modern Periodic Table

The periodic table is organized into **periods** (horizontal rows) and **groups** (vertical columns).

- Seven periods correspond to the seven principal electron shells
- Elements in the same group share valence-electron counts and chemical behaviour

blocks.title: Listening: Periodic trends

citations.excerpt: Atomic Radius: Decreases across a period (left to right) because the increasing nuclear charge pulls electrons closer. It increases down a group because new shells are added.

blocks.text: As we move across a period from left to right, the atomic radius decreases, because the growing nuclear charge pulls the electrons closer. Moving down a group, new shells are added and the radius increases.

citations.excerpt: The Atomic Number is the number of protons in the nucleus of an atom. It is the fundamental property that defines an element.

questions.prompt: What property orders the elements in the modern periodic table?
questions.feedback: Moseley showed the elements should be ordered by atomic number — the number of protons — not atomic mass.

questions.prompt: What are the horizontal rows of the periodic table called?
questions.feedback: The horizontal rows are called periods; the vertical columns are groups.

blocks.title: Blocks of the periodic table

citations.excerpt: The table is further divided into blocks based on orbital filling: s-block: Groups 1 and 2 (and Helium). p-block: Groups 13 to 18. d-block: Transition metals (Groups 3 to 12).

blocks.svg: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 400 200" role="img" aria-label="Blocks of the periodic table"><title>Blocks of the periodic table</title><desc>Four labelled regions showing the s, p, d and f blocks.</desc><rect x="10" y="10" width="80" height="120" fill="#cde"/><text x="30" y="70" font-size="16">s</text><rect x="250" y="10" width="140" height="120" fill="#dec"/><text x="310" y="70" font-size="16">p</text><rect x="100" y="40" width="140" height="90" fill="#edc"/><text x="160" y="90" font-size="16">d</text><rect x="60" y="150" width="220" height="40" fill="#dce"/><text x="160" y="175" font-size="16">f</text></svg>
blocks.altText: Diagram of the periodic table divided into s, p, d and f blocks.
blocks.longDescription: The s-block covers Groups 1 and 2 plus Helium; the p-block covers Groups 13 to 18; the d-block covers the transition metals in Groups 3 to 12; the f-block holds the lanthanides and actinides below the main table.
blocks.caption: The four orbital-filling blocks of the periodic table.

citations.excerpt: Ionic compounds do not exist as discrete molecules but as giant three-dimensional crystal lattices.

poster.altText: A cubic sodium chloride crystal lattice with alternating sodium and chloride ions.
poster.longDescription: Alternating sodium cations and chloride anions arranged in a repeating cubic lattice, each ion surrounded by six of the opposite charge.
poster.caption: The NaCl lattice: alternating Na+ and Cl− ions in a cubic arrangement.

annotations.text: Sodium cation (Na+) — lost one valence electron.
Chloride anion (Cl−) — gained one electron to complete its octet.

blocks.title: Ionic bonding: electron transfer

citations.excerpt: Ionic bonding is the electrostatic force of attraction between oppositely charged ions formed by the complete transfer of one or more electrons from a metal atom to a non-metal atom.

blocks.name: Electron transfer
blocks.paramsDescription: One slider controls how many electrons transfer from the metal atom to the non-metal atom (1 to 3). Transferring more electrons produces more strongly charged ions.

poster.altText: A sodium atom transferring one electron to a chlorine atom.
poster.caption: Electron transfer between a metal and a non-metal forms oppositely charged ions.
